title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.
question: quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" ècrit par Ozan Caglayan et al., quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the
output_text: absence of appropriate visual context in the training data. In this paper, we explore the need for visual context in MMT by conducting a series of experiments with different hyperparameters. We consider three types of visual context: (1) image captions, (2) image tags, and (3) image regions. We also investigate the impact of different types of visual context on the performance of MMT models.The hyperparameters chosen for the experiments include the following:1. Image captions: We use image captions as the visual context for the MMT models. We experiment with different lengths of captions, ranging from 1 to 5 sentences.2. Image tags: We use image tags as the visual context for the MMT models. We experiment with different types of tags, such as object tags, scene tags, and attribute tags.3. Image regions: We use image regions as the visual context for the MMT models. We experiment with different sizes of regions, ranging from 100 to 500 pixels.In addition to these hyperparameters, we also experiment with different MMT models, including a baseline model and a state-of-the-art model. We evaluate the performance of the models using various metrics, such as BLEU, METEOR, and ROUGE.Overall, our experiments suggest that the use of visual context can significantly improve the performance of MMT models. However, the choice of hyperparameters and the type of visual context used can have a significant impact on the performance of the models. Further research is needed to determine the optimal combination of hyperparameters and visual context for MMT.

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.
question: Comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" ècrit par Alon Talmor et al., comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some rel
output_text: ation to a text passage, but this approach ignores the importance of commonsense knowledge in understanding the world. To address this gap, Talmor et al. propose CommonsenseQA, a question answering challenge that focuses on testing the ability of models to reason about commonsense knowledge.To test the human performance, the authors conducted a study with 1,000 participants who were asked to answer a set of 100 questions from the CommonsenseQA dataset. The questions were designed to test the participants' ability to reason about commonsense knowledge, and the answers were provided in the form of multiple-choice options. The participants were given 10 seconds to answer each question.The results of the study showed that humans performed well on the CommonsenseQA dataset, with an average accuracy of 75.5%. However, there was a significant variation in performance across different questions, with some questions being answered correctly by more than 90% of participants, while others were answered correctly by less than 50% of participants.The authors also found that the performance of humans on the CommonsenseQA dataset was significantly better than that of state-of-the-art machine learning models, which achieved an average accuracy of only 57.5%. This suggests that there is still a lot of work to be done in developing models that can reason about commonsense knowledge as effectively as humans.

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.
question: Quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" ècrit par Nitika Mathur et al., quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard o
output_text: f human evaluation is a crucial task. In the article "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" written by Nitika Mathur et al., les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l'évaluation sont :1. BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) : Il s'agit d'un des métriques les plus couramment utilisées pour évaluer la qualité des traductions automatisées. BLEU calcule la similarité entre la traduction automatique et les traductions humaines de référence en prenant en compte la longueur des phrases et la similarité des mots.2. METEOR (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering) : Il s'agit d'un autre métrique qui prend en compte l'ordre des mots et des phrases dans la traduction automatique. METEOR est particulièrement utile pour les traductions où l'ordre des mots est important, comme dans les traductions de textes techniques ou scientifiques.3. ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) : Il s'agit d'un métrique qui évalue la qualité des traductions en se concentrant sur la compréhension globale du texte. ROUGE prend en compte la similarité entre la traduction automatique et les traductions humaines de référence en termes de contenu global, sans se soucier de l'ordre des mots ou des phrases.L'article de Mathur et al. examine en détail ces trois mesures de base et souligne les forces et les faiblesses de chacune d'elles. Ils concluent que, bien que BLEU soit largement utilisé, il peut être biaisé et ne prend pas en compte certains aspects importants de la qualité de la traduction, tels que la cohérence et la pertinence. METEOR et ROUGE offrent des alternatives plus complètes, mais elles ont également leurs propres limitations.

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.
question: Quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times" ècrit par Andrea de Varda et al., quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predicti
output_text: ve of human behavior. However, these models often lack the ability to capture the complex cognitive processes involved in reading comprehension. In this article, the authors propose a multilingual approach to human reading times that incorporates eye movement data to better understand the cognitive processes involved in reading comprehension.The authors use a dataset of eye movement data collected from participants reading a variety of texts in different languages. This dataset allows them to investigate the relationship between eye movements and reading times, and to develop a model that can predict reading times based on eye movement data.The authors find that eye movement data can provide valuable insights into the cognitive processes involved in reading comprehension, and that incorporating this data into computational models can improve their accuracy. They also find that the multilingual approach they propose can be used to study reading comprehension in a variety of languages, and that it can be used to develop models that are more accurate and more generalizable than existing models.Overall, the article provides a valuable contribution to the field of computational psycholinguistics, and it demonstrates the potential of incorporating eye movement data into computational models of reading comprehension.

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.
question: utilisées pour les expériences ?quels sont les deux types de modèles pour lesquels les auteurs ont présenté les résultats ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "UnNatural Language Inference" ècrit par Koustuv Sinha et al., utilisées pour les expériences ?quels sont les deux types de modèles pour lesquels les auteurs ont présenté les résultats ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to unders
output_text: Dans l'article "UnNatural Language Inference" écrit par Koustuv Sinha et al., les auteurs ont présenté les résultats pour deux types de modèles : les modèles de type "UnNatural Language Inference" (U-NLI) et les modèles de type "Natural Language Inference" (NLI). Les deux types de modèles ont été utilisés pour les expériences menées dans l'article.Les modèles U-NLI sont basés sur les modèles de NLI, mais ils ont été entraînés sur des données d'inférence naturelle modifiées pour inclure des exemples d'inférence non naturelle. Les auteurs ont utilisé ces modèles pour étudier comment les modèles de NLI se comportent face à des exemples d'inférence non naturelle et comment cela peut affecter leur performance globale.Les modèles NLI, quant à eux, sont des modèles de NLU qui ont été entraînés sur des données d'inférence naturelle. Les auteurs ont utilisé ces modèles pour comparer leur performance avec celle des modèles U-NLI et pour étudier comment les modèles de NLI peuvent être améliorés pour gérer les exemples d'inférence non naturelle.En résumé, les deux types de modèles utilisés dans l'article sont les modèles U-NLI et les modèles NLI, et les auteurs ont présenté les résultats pour ces deux types de modèles dans le cadre de leurs expériences.

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.
question: quel est le nom du corpus en anglais utilisé pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP" ècrit par Richeek Das et al., quel est le nom du corpus en anglais utilisé pour les expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the wo
output_text: rld's languages, code-switched text is less abundant. In this paper, we present a new corpus of code-switched text, called the "Code-Switched English-Hindi (CSEH) Corpus", which we use to evaluate the performance of pretrained models on code-switched NLP tasks. We also propose a new pretraining technique, called "Code-Switched Pretraining", which leverages the unique characteristics of code-switched text to improve the performance of pretrained models on code-switched NLP tasks. Our experiments show that Code-Switched Pretraining outperforms existing pretraining techniques on code-switched NLP tasks, and that the CSEH Corpus is a valuable resource for evaluating the performance of pretrained models on code-switched NLP tasks.

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.
question: Quelles sont les limites de l'article ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles" ècrit par Pietro Lesci et al., quelles sont les limites de l'article ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines me
output_text: mory profiles as the ability of a model to memorize a specific input, but these definitions are often ad-hoc and lack a clear theoretical foundation. In this paper, we propose a causal framework for estimating memorisation profiles, which allows us to quantify the memorisation ability of a model in a principled and interpretable way. Our framework is based on the idea that memorisation can be seen as a causal effect of the model's training process, and we provide a method for estimating this causal effect using a counterfactual analysis. We evaluate our framework on a variety of language models and show that it provides a more accurate and interpretable measure of memorisation than existing methods.Les limites de l'article sont les suivantes :1. La méthode de l'article repose sur l'hypothèse que la mémorisation peut être considérée comme un effet causal du processus d'entraînement du modèle. Cette hypothèse n'est pas nécessairement vraie pour tous les types de modèles ou de données, et il est possible que d'autres approches causales puissent être utilisées pour estimer les profils de mémorisation.2. L'article ne fournit pas de comparaison avec d'autres méthodes pour estimer les profils de mémorisation, ce qui rend difficile de déterminer si la méthode proposée est la meilleure ou la plus adaptée à un certain type de modèle ou de données.3. L'article ne discute pas des implications éthiques ou sociales de l'estimation des profils de mémorisation, ce qui peut être important pour comprendre comment ces mesures peuvent être utilisées de manière responsable.4. L'article ne fournit pas de preuve de l'utilité de l'estimation des profils de mémorisation pour résoudre des problèmes pratiques ou sociaux, tels que la prévention de l'infringement de copyright ou l'étude des dynamiques d'entraînement des modèles.

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.
question: Quelle est la direction future de la recherche mentionnée par les auteurs ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them" ècrit par Dawid Jurkiewicz et al., quelle est la direction future de la recherche mentionnée par les auteurs ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of
output_text: l'article est de présenter les résultats de la participation de l'équipe ApplicaAI à la tâche 11 de SemEval-2020, qui consiste à classer les techniques de propagande et à identifier les spans de texte contenant des techniques de propagande. Les auteurs ont utilisé plusieurs modèles de langage, notamment RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS et un modèle auto-entraîné, pour évaluer leur performance sur cette tâche.Dans leur conclusion, les auteurs mentionnent que la direction future de la recherche dans ce domaine pourrait être axée sur l'amélioration des modèles de langage pour la classification et l'identification de techniques de propagande, en utilisant des données plus grandes et plus diversifiées, ainsi que des méthodes d'entraînement plus sophistiquées. Ils soulignent également l'importance de la transparence et de l'interprétabilité des modèles de langage, afin de permettre aux utilisateurs de comprendre comment ces modèles prennent leurs décisions et de s'assurer qu'ils sont justes et équitables.En résumé, la direction future de la recherche dans ce domaine pourrait être axée sur l'amélioration des modèles de langage pour la classification et l'identification de techniques de propagande, en utilisant des données plus grandes et plus diversifiées, ainsi que des méthodes d'entraînement plus sophistiquées, tout en mettant l'accent sur la transparence et l'interprétabilité des modèles.

title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.
question: À partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology" ècrit par Marcel Bollmann et al., à partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexi
output_text: ty of the word. We conduct experiments on two datasets, one with simple morphology and one with complex morphology, and find that the conjectures hold for both datasets. Our results suggest that morphology is an important factor in error analysis and that its importance increases with the complexity of the word.

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.
question: Dans quel but pré-entraînent-ils un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data" ècrit par Emily M. Bender et al., dans quel but pré-entraînent-ils un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “u
output_text: sually intelligent” or “human-like” in their understanding of language. In reality, these models are still far from achieving human-level understanding of language. In the article "Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data" written by Emily M. Bender et al., the authors pre-train a model for a binary prediction of the sentence "The cat is on the mat" in order to demonstrate the limitations of current language models and to emphasize the importance of understanding the meaning and form of language for achieving human-level natural language understanding (NLU).

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).
question: Quels ensembles de données de sourcing ont-ils utilisés pour la flagornerie ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition" ècrit par Nina Rimsky et al., quels ensembles de données de sourcing ont-ils utilisés pour la flagornerie ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averagin
output_text: g the activations of the language model's output layer for different input tokens. These steering vectors are then used to modify the activations of the language model during the forward pass. We evaluate CAA on the Steering Llama 2 dataset, which consists of 100,000 human-written prompts and 100,000 generated responses. We find that CAA significantly improves the performance of the language model on the Steering Llama 2 dataset, and we provide detailed analysis of the results.

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.
question: Quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant" ècrit par Pollawat Hongwimol et al., quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in underst
output_text: dans les résultats de recherche. Les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé sont les suivantes :1. Explication des résultats de recherche : ESRA fournit des explications détaillées pour chaque résultat de recherche, aidant les utilisateurs à comprendre les concepts clés et les relations entre les termes de recherche.2. Augmentation des résultats de recherche : ESRA augmente les résultats de recherche avec des informations pertinentes et des détails supplémentaires, tels que des citations, des images et des vidéos, pour aider les utilisateurs à mieux comprendre les sujets de recherche.3. Personnalisation des résultats de recherche : ESRA prend en compte les préférences et les intérêts des utilisateurs pour fournir des résultats de recherche personnalisés et pertinents, en utilisant des techniques de filtrage et de classification avancées.

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.
question: y
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application" ècrit par Amit Moryossef, y Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source
output_text: technologies, sign.mt aims to bridge the communication gap between deaf and hearing individuals by providing a seamless and accurate translation experience. The application utilizes deep learning algorithms and neural networks to analyze and translate sign language gestures into spoken language and vice versa.sign.mt supports multiple sign languages, including American Sign Language (ASL), British Sign Language (BSL), and French Sign Language (LSF), among others. The application also offers a multilingual translation feature, allowing users to communicate in their preferred language, regardless of their location or cultural background.The paper discusses the challenges and limitations of current sign language translation systems and highlights the potential impact of sign.mt in promoting inclusivity and accessibility for deaf individuals. The authors emphasize the importance of open-source technology in making sign language translation more accessible and affordable for a wider audience.In conclusion, sign.mt represents a significant step forward in the field of sign language translation, offering a reliable and user-friendly solution for bridging the communication gap between deaf and hearing individuals. The open-source nature of the application ensures that it remains accessible and adaptable to the evolving needs of the deaf community.

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.
question: quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck" ècrit par Xiang Lisa Li et al., quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variatio
output_text: Dans l'article intitulé "Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck" écrit par Xiang Lisa Li et al., ils ont utilisé l'optimiseur de la fonction d'information pour leurs expériences. La fonction d'information est une mesure de la complexité de l'information dans un système, et elle est utilisée pour optimiser les paramètres du modèle. L'article propose une méthode de spécialisation des émbeddings de mots basée sur l'information bottleneck, qui permet de réduire la complexité de l'information tout en conservant la performance sur diverses tâches de traitement du langage naturel.

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.
question: Comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation" ècrit par Jean Maillard et al., comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmente
output_text: d avec des données collectées à partir de sources diverses. Cependant, ces données peuvent être de qualité variable et ne refléter pas toujours la diversité linguistique et culturelle des populations cibles. Dans leur article "Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation", Jean Maillard et al. ont cherché à identifier les langues pour lesquelles la collecte de données est la plus difficile et pour lesquelles les méthodes de collecte de données minimalistes pourraient être les plus utiles.Pour sélectionner les langues, ils ont utilisé plusieurs critères. Tout d'abord, ils ont identifié les langues les moins représentées dans les bases de données de traduction existantes, telles que Europarl, OPUS et WMT. Ensuite, ils ont pris en compte la difficulté de collecte de données pour ces langues, en tenant compte de facteurs tels que la taille de la population parlant la langue, la disponibilité de ressources linguistiques et la diversité linguistique et culturelle.Enfin, ils ont sélectionné des langues pour lesquelles les méthodes de collecte de données minimalistes pourraient être les plus utiles, telles que les langues avec des ressources linguistiques limitées, des langues avec des dialectes ou des variétés régionales, ou des langues avec des caractéristiques linguistiques spécifiques qui pourraient être difficiles à capturer avec des données traditionnelles.En résumé, Jean Maillard et al. ont sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données en utilisant une combinaison de critères tels que la représentation dans les bases de données de traduction existantes, la difficulté de collecte de données et l'utilité potentielle des méthodes de collecte de données minimalistes.

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4

However, this has also raised concerns about the poten
output_text: tial misuse of LLMs for malicious purposes, such as generating fake news, spam, or even deepfake videos. To address this issue, researchers have developed various methods to detect machine-generated text (MGT) and distinguish it from human-generated text (HGT).In the article "M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection" by Yuxia Wang et al., the authors propose a new black-box machine-generated text detection (MGD) model called M4. The M4 model is designed to detect machine-generated text in multiple domains and languages, making it a versatile tool for detecting MGT.The M4 model is based on a multi-generator architecture, which means it can detect machine-generated text generated by different models. It also uses a multi-domain approach, which means it can detect machine-generated text in various domains such as news, social media, and scientific papers. Finally, the M4 model is designed to be multi-lingual, which means it can detect machine-generated text in multiple languages.The authors evaluate the performance of the M4 model on various datasets and compare it with other state-of-the-art MGD models. The results show that the M4 model outperforms other models in terms of detection accuracy and robustness.In conclusion, the M4 model proposed by Yuxia Wang et al. is a promising black-box machine-generated text detection model that can detect machine-generated text in multiple domains and languages. Its multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual architecture make it a versatile tool for detecting machine-generated text and preventing its misuse.

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.

output_text: eached human-level performance on various NLP tasks. These models have revolutionized the field of speech translation, enabling the development of advanced speech-to-text and text-to-speech systems. However, there are still several challenges and limitations that need to be addressed to fully exploit the potential of these models.Le contexte de cet article est donc centré sur la traduction automatique de la parole, en particulier la traduction de la parole en texte et du texte en parole. L'article examine les fondations des modèles de parole et les grands modèles de langage, ainsi que les défis et les lacunes qui subsistent dans ce domaine.

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.

title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

L'article présente disco, un outil de contrôle distributionnel pour les modèles génératifs, qui permet de contrôler et d'évaluer les biases présents dans les données d'entraînement des modèles.

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.

Le premier réseau convolutif est utilisé pour extraire des caractéristiques acoustiques des données audio, tandis que le second réseau convolutif est utilisé pour générer des représentations phonologiques des données audio.<|im_end|>

title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.
question: Comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" ècrit par Alon Talmor et al., comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
output_text: Dans l'article "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" écrit par Alon Talmor et al., la précision humaine a été testée en demandant à un groupe de 100 participants de répondre à 100 questions de connaissances communes. Les participants ont été invités à fournir une réponse pour chaque question, et leurs réponses ont été comparées à celles fournies par un modèle de langage pré-entraîné. Le modèle a été évalué en calculant la précision de ses réponses par rapport aux réponses fournies par les participants. Les résultats ont montré que le modèle avait une précision de 80,7% par rapport aux réponses fournies par les participants, ce qui indique que le modèle était capable de fournir des réponses précises à des questions de connaissances communes.<|im_end|>

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.

output_text: Les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l'évaluation dans l'article "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" sont la précision, le rappel et la F1-score. Ces mesures sont couramment utilisées pour évaluer la qualité de la traduction automatique et sont largement acceptées dans la communauté de la traduction automatique.<|im_end|>

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.

output_text: Les données de mouvements oculaires utilisées dans l'article sont connues sous le nom de "Eye Tracking Data". Elles sont collectées à l'aide d'un dispositif de suivi oculaire qui enregistre les mouvements des yeux d'un individu pendant qu'il lit un texte. Ces données sont ensuite analysées pour fournir des informations sur les processus cognitifs impliqués dans la lecture.<|im_end|>

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.

prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "UnNatural Language Inference" ècrit par Koustuv Sinha et al., utilisées pour les expériences ?quels sont les deux types de modèles pour lesquels les auteurs ont présenté les résultats ?

Les modèles de régression logistique sont utilisés pour prédire la probabilité d'une classe donnée, tandis que les modèles de régression linéaire sont utilisés pour prédire une valeur numérique.<|im_end|>

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.
question: Quelles sont les limites de l'article ?

output_text: L'article "Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles" écrit par Pietro Lesci et al. présente une méthode pour estimer les profils de mémorisation en utilisant des données d'expériences de mémoire. Cependant, il y a plusieurs limites à cette méthode.Tout d'abord, la méthode repose sur l'hypothèse que les données d'expériences de mémoire sont représentatives de la population générale. Cependant, il est possible que les données ne soient pas représentatives de la population générale, ce qui pourrait entraîner des estimations biaisées des profils de mémorisation.Deuxièmement, la méthode repose sur l'hypothèse que les données d'expériences de mémoire sont suffisamment précises pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation. Cependant, il est possible que les données ne soient pas suffisamment précises pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation, ce qui pourrait entraîner des estimations imprécises.Troisièmement, la méthode repose sur l'hypothèse que les données d'expériences de mémoire sont suffisamment complètes pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation. Cependant, il est possible que les données ne soient pas suffisamment complètes pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation, ce qui pourrait entraîner des estimations incomplètes.Enfin, la méthode repose sur l'hypothèse que les données d'expériences de mémoire sont suffisamment fiables pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation. Cependant, il est possible que les données ne soient pas suffisamment fiables pour permettre une estimation précise des profils de mémorisation, ce qui pourrait entraîner des estimations inexactes.<|im_end|>

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.

De plus, l'utilisation de techniques d'apprentissage par renforcement pour améliorer les performances des modèles de traitement du langage naturel pourrait également être explorée.<|im_end|>

title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.

output_text: Dans l'article intitulé "Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology" écrit par Marcel Bollmann et al., les auteurs ont sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences en fonction des tâches partagées. title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.
question: Dans quel but pré-entraînent-ils un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante ?

output_text: Dans l'article "Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data" écrit par Emily M. Bender et al., ils pré-entraînent un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante afin de déterminer si la phrase suivante est une question ou non. Cela permet au modèle de mieux comprendre le contexte et la structure des phrases, ce qui peut améliorer sa capacité à effectuer des tâches de traitement du langage naturel.<|im_end|>

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.

title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.
