title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.
question: quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" ècrit par Ozan Caglayan et al., quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the
output_text: niveau de détail élevé des données d'entraînement, l'utilisation de modèles de langage pré-entraînés et l'utilisation de techniques d'apprentissage profond qui sont capables d'apprendre à manipuler les données visuelles. En conséquence, les systèmes sont capables de traduire les images en paroles sans aucun gain de précision substantiel. Cependant, nous suggérons que le gain de précision peut être obtenu en abordant les sous-problèmes spécifiques découlant du fait que l'intégration de la modalité visuelle dans le MMT est plus difficile que l'intégration de la modalité textuelle. Pour tester cette hypothèse, nous discutons des abandons de certaines techniques d'apprentissage profond dans les expériences avec des modèles MMT. Nous discutons de l'utilisation de l'abandon RNN, du champs de marque contrôlée (CMAC) et de l'hyperparamètre ABL du réseau de neurones récurrents (RNN) pour éviter la surpassivation des champs de marque. Nous discutons également de l'utilisation du délai de transfert pour réduire la divergence de référence dans les méthodes de régression de sortie basées sur la représentation de motif et les données de champs de marque. Nous discutons ensuite de l'état de l'art des méthodes de régression de sortie basées sur la représentation de motif qui utilisent des données de champs de marque pour s'aligner avec des traductions sous-optimales et pour éviter la divergence de référence. Enfin, nous discutons de l'utilisation de la technique de l'abandon en temps réel pour éviter la divergence de référence dans les méthodes de régression de sortie basées sur la représentation de motif qui utilisent des données de champs de marque pour prévoir les traductions.<|im_end|>

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.
question: Comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" ècrit par Alon Talmor et al., comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some rel
output_text: context (e.g. facts) or limited text. However, knowledge of common-sense facts is required for answering a large fraction of questions in open-domain QA and, thus, should be considered in answer generating procedures. In this work, we address this issue by proposing a new benchmark dataset, commonsenseQA, that consists of questions requiring answers that are known to most individuals and whose resolution is not based on general knowledge or world understanding. In order to test the difficulty of our dataset, we compare it with other open-domain QA datasets and show that these facts are not present in other datasets. Thus, our dataset allows us to evaluate and compare the performance of previous work and new methods, where most answers are based on commonsense knowledge. We show that our dataset is challenging for a wide range of state-of-the-art models and that the best models on our dataset are far from the human level. Furthermore, we show that simple fact completion techniques, which are a common method used in open-domain factoid QA, do not yield significant improvement to our results. Our dataset is publicly available on GitHub. We report and discuss the main results achieved by our models and provide a comprehensive analysis of the difficulty of our dataset based on human performance on a subset of the questions."<|im_end|>

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.
question: Quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" ècrit par Nitika Mathur et al., quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard o
output_text: s trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation sont la mesure BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy), la mesure n-gramme et la mesure caractère. Ces mesures sont utilisées pour évaluer la qualité de la traduction automatique en comparant le texte original avec le texte traduit. Les mesures BLEU et n-gramme mesurent la proximité des mots et des phrases entre le texte original et le texte traduit, tandis que la mesure caractère mesure la précision et la lisibilité du texte traduit.<|im_end|>

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.
question: Quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times" ècrit par Andrea de Varda et al., quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predicti
output_text: sive of the historical context, the type of text, genre, and language. However, although they have been used to infer the probability of finding a particular word in a text given a set of textual contextual features, these models have not been used to predict reading times as functions of contexts, in spite of the fact that context affects the time taken to read a word. Here we explore the feasibility and scalability of using multiple neural language models to predict reading times as a function of lexical, contextual, and semantic features, as well as their limitations. We evaluate these approaches on two datasets, one in English and the other in German, and compare their performance to the performance of a standard logistic regression model that includes contextual and semantic information. We further use these models to predict reading time given multiple contextual and semantic features and determine the relative importance of the features in predicting reading times for each language.Les données de mouvements oculaires mentionnées dans l'article sont le TED Reading Benchmark (TEDRB) et l'AIDA Database.Le TED Reading Benchmark (TEDRB) est une collection de données de mouvements oculaires enregistrés lors de la lecture de tâches de compréhension de texte complexes. Ces données sont disponibles en anglais, en allemand et en espagnol.L'AIDA Database est un ensemble de données de mouvements oculaires et de temps de réponse enregistrés lors de la lecture de tâches de compréhension de texte à des niveaux de difficulté variables. Ces données sont disponibles en anglais, en allemand et en italien.Dans l'article, les auteurs ont utilisé des mesures de temps de lecture et des métriques d'engagement oculaire pour évaluer la performance de différents types de modèles de langage neuronal, dont le logistic regression, pour prédire le temps de lecture en fonction de paramètres linguistiques, contextuels et sémantiques.Les résultats ont montré que l'utilisation de plusieurs modèles de langage neuronal pour prédire le temps de lecture était efficace pour le TEDRB en anglais, mais moins effic

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to unders
output_text: alyser des données textuelles plus spécifiquement (par exemple, des phrases, des idiomes et des questions à choix multiples). Dans cette analyse, nous avons développé une nouvelle méthode appelée unNatural Language Inference (unNLI), qui tente de reproduire ce comportement dans des tâches naturelles simples en utilisant des modèles de langage pré-entraînés. Pour chaque tâche donnée, nous avons défini un ensemble de paires de phrases ("première phrase" et "seconde phrase") où la relation entre les deux phrases doit être prédite par un modèle. Nous avons ensuite entraîné les modèles pour prédire la relation entre les phrases, et avons évalué leur performance en utilisant un ensemble de méthodes statistiques. Sur la base de notre expérience, nous avons constaté que la performance des modèles sur des tâches simples a été améliorée grâce à cette approche, ce qui est une avancée significative sur les approches existantes.<|im_end|>

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.
question: quel est le nom du corpus en anglais utilisé pour les expériences ?

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the wo
output_text: s major languages, there is a paucity of such resources in many under-resourced languages. To address this problem, we propose to use a combination of monolingual and bilingual data for pretraining in an encoder-decoder style model without any explicit translation. We tested this approach on the i2b2 code-switched clinical notes corpus, which spans 300,000 words across a bilingual (English-Spanish) corpus. Our code-switched word embeddings capture key similarities between the two languages, and lead to better Named Entity Recognition on both languages than the monolingual embeddings or those learned purely from the cross-lingual context. When combined with a multilingual corpus, our results also outperform the BERT-Base model on monolingual English Named Entity Recognition. We conclude that combining bilingual and monolingual pretrained word embeddings is a good approach to handling low-resource languages in the NLP domain. "<|im_end|>

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.

L'article propose de définir la capacité et le rythme de mémorisation d'un modèle comme les données qui lui sont présentées. En utilisant des données provenant de divers travaux relatifs à la mémoire humaine, l'article construit un modèle de mémoire avec des données et des contraintes qui n'étaient pas présentes dans les modèles empiriques. Ces contraintes sont liées à la sphère cognitive et aux processus neuronaux.<|im_end|>

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.

output_text: article est de discuter de la performance de la tâche de classification des techniques de propagande (TC) et de la tâche d'identification des ensembles de propagande (SI) à SemEval-2020, ainsi que de présenter les systèmes gagnants pour ces tâches. title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.
question: À partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology" ècrit par Marcel Bollmann et al., à partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexi
output_text: té of the word (i.e., with length). We conduct error analysis experiments on four standard tasks with English and German data sets using both human and automatic annotations. We analyze the errors along different dimensions: i.e., in terms of their part-of-speech (POS), lemma, and morphological attributes: i.e., stems and affixes on the morpheme, word, stem, and surface level.Les auteurs ont sélectionné les ensembles de données appropriés pour leurs expériences de recherche en fonction de tâches partagées telles que le nommage d'entités nommées (NER), l'appariement, la rétrogradation, la segmentation de mots et la classification NLP. Ils ont utilisé l'évaluation manuelle et automatisée pour analyser les erreurs dans différentes dimensions, telles que le POS, le lématiage et les attributs morphologiques.<|im_end|>

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.
question: Dans quel but pré-entraînent-ils un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data" ècrit par Emily M. Bender et al., dans quel but pré-entraînent-ils un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “u
output_text: lminating the black box of linguistics” or as if NLP research is essentially “done” now.” This hype may confuse the public, lead to extrapolation to tasks that are not known to work and may mislead the ML community, NLP researchers, and practitioners. In this work, we will propose a series of experiments and discussions of NLU tasks that can potentially expose potential flaws and limitations in these models, and we will conclude by suggesting some approaches to use for the future development in NLTK. In this paper, we will also present some examples of work in progress on the development of these models. En somme, ils pré-entraînent un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante dans le but de vérifier les succès des modèles de NLP et d'identifier les potentielles failles ou limites de ces modèles, tout en suggérant des approches pour les modèles à venir dans NLTK.<|im_end|>

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averagin
output_text: lizing the differences between the activation distributions of the source and target text prompts.CAA was evaluated on a set of 15 datasets for textual entailment as well as a set of manually-curated language tasks. On the textual entailment and SQuAD tasks, CAA significantly improved the results for steering tasks compared to other methods such as Beam Search and Decode and Re-Decode.In addition, CAA was also evaluated on a set of manually-curated language tasks, including translation, grammatical error detection, and sentiment analysis. Similar to the results on textual entailment tasks, CAA significantly improved the results on these tasks compared to other methods such as Beam Search and Decode and Re-Decode.Overall, CAA demonstrated to be an effective method for steering language models and achieved state-of-the-art or close-to-state-of-the-art results on various language tasks.Source:https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.12080.pdf<|im_end|>

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.
question: Quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant" ècrit par Pollawat Hongwimol et al., quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in underst
output_text: tant les implications of research. The architecture of ESRA integrates three essential components for delivering explainable results: 1) a text mining component to generate an abstract, 2) a knowledge reasoning component to explain the key findings, and 3) a visualization component to enable better exploration of the research. ESRA is evaluated using a dataset of published abstracts and papers in academic digital libraries, including PubMed, PMC, and Google Scholar. Results show that ESRA is effective in generating explainable results for discovering relevant information to advance scientific literacy and research productivity. La partie du résumé de l'article donné est déjà en anglais, je ne peux pas traduire cette phrase.<|im_end|>

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.

L'utilisation de sources open-source de pointe permet une traduction en temps réel de la langue des signes. La traduction en temps réel de la langue des signes peut être utilisée pour faciliter la communication entre les personnes sourdes et entendantes, ainsi que pour améliorer les soins de santé et les services publics pour les personnes sourdes ou malentendantes.L'application exploite des techniques d'apprentissage automatique et d'intelligence artificielle pour fournir une traduction précise et efficace des langues des signes en temps réel. Elle est accessible aux développeurs intéressés par cette technologie et peut être adaptée à leurs besoins spécifiques. L'application a été développée dans plusieurs langues, dont l'anglais, le français, l'espagnol, l'arabe et le chinois.Dans l'ensemble, l'application sign.mt offre une solution pratique et accessible pour améliorer la communication entre les personnes sourdes et entendantes. Elle contribue à la mission de rendre les langues des signes accessibles à tous.<|im_end|>

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.
question: quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck" ècrit par Xiang Lisa Li et al., quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variatio
output_text: n over Gromov-Wasscerstein (GW), a direct solution for variational inference. We present a proof that shows the upper bound for the GW distance, which leads to a more efficient implementations of the GIB optimizer than an existing method. Our main contributions are an alternative optimizer for information bottleneck called GIB that outperforms the previous work in terms of both computational costs and speed. GIB is highly efficient even for complex models. We demonstrate the performance of GIB on four synthetic datasets and three real-world tasks. Experimental results show that word embedding models combined with GIB outperform models based on other existing optimizers.<|im_end|>

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.
question: Comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation" ècrit par Jean Maillard et al., comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmente
output_text: d by small amount of data. This approach, however, has drawbacks: It only works for source-target language pairs used in the datasets, which restricts the scope of applications where models can be trained. We propose a different approach by using small datasets for language pairs not included in existing datasets. We select the languages using five quantitative characteristics: (a) the ratio of speakers with a native language other than the majority language in a region, (b) the linguistic distance between majority and minority language, (c) the number of minority languages whose majority languages are different, (d) the available annotated domain corpora, and (e) the population of the region. Through this approach, we select languages for which translation is not yet well established, but for which the potential impact is high, as well as the need for efficient translation is. The resulting small datasets were collected from crowdsourcing for diverse domains and translated from two languages: French and Indonesian. This approach allows us to create additional pairs for machine translation systems, and to adapt machine translation systems to different contexts. For example, when translating between languages with high linguistic distance, small datasets can be augmented with domain corpora as well as parallel datasets. When translating for diverse domains, we can augment small datasets with domain-specific corpora to improve the quality of translation. Finally, if we plan to have many parallel translations, the approach allows us to create a translation pipeline specific to diverse contexts. The paper provides a detailed analysis of the approach, as well as empirical results showing increased quality compared to existing baselines. The study is a significant contribution to the field of machine translation and demonstrates that even with minimal data, one can effectively train machine translation systems for various contexts.<|im_end|>

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4
question: quels sont les détecteurs Ope
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection" ècrit par Yuxia Wang et al., quels sont les détecteurs Ope Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the poten
output_text: cial for its output quality. While existing methods have been adopted to evaluate and improve generative models, they mainly focus on the detection of natural text. On the other hand, there is a growing demand for ML/AI researchers to address the problem of black-box machine-generated text detctores. In this article, the authors introduce a novel multi-generator framework for detecting machine-generated text. The framework, which is called the Multi-generator Multidomain Multilangaual (M4) model, is based on the idea of using different generators to learn different aspects of the data, and then combining these learnings to create a more robust detector.The M4 model utilizes a combination of two types of generators: a text generator and a domain generator. The text generator is responsible for generating the target text, while the domain generator is responsible for generating the domain of the target text. The combination of these two generators enables the M4 model to detect black-box machine-generated text with higher accuracy.In addition, the M4 model utilizes a multi-lingual approach for detecting machine-generated text. This approach enables the M4 model to detect machine-generated text in multiple languages. The M4 model also utilizes a multi-modal framework to detect machine-generated text. This framework enables the M4 model to detect machine-generated text in both audio and visual forms.The authors conducted extensive experiments to evaluate the effectiveness of the M4 model. The experiments showed that the M4 model achieved significantly higher accuracies than existing detection methods. The accuracy of the M4 model was consistently around 95%, even when it was tested on text containing multiple domains and languages.Overall, the M4 model offers a promising new approach for detecting machine-generated text. The multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual framework of the M4 model enables it to detect machine-generated text with higher accuracy than existing methods. Additionally, the multi-modal framework of the M4 model enables it to detect machine-generated text in both audio and visual forms.<|im_end|>

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.
question: à quel contexte ou à quelle tâche se limite ce survey ?

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have r
output_text: e large impact on speech translation tasks. In this article, we analyze the state of the art in speech translation research and the key challenges in this field. We discuss how LLMs are being used to improve speech translation performance and discuss the potential of foundation models for this task. Our analysis reveals the current state of the field and offers insights into how to address the challenges facing this important research area. A detailed survey of methods and approaches in speech translation is presented. We highlight the benefits and drawbacks of different models and methods, and summarize key recent advances. The article concludes with a discussion of the potential of foundation models for this task and their impact on future research directions in speech translation."<|im_end|>

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.

In this paper, we present WikiBio, a semantic resource for intersecting analysis of biographical events. WikiBio is a set of data derived from Wikipedia biographies, processed to obtain event information. It includes about 1.2 million event data, organized in 300,000 bios for 100,000 people from different language editions. This article reports on the architecture and processing techniques used to construct WikiBio, focusing on the identification and classification of biographical events. We also highlight its potential applications for automatic and semi-automatic classification tasks, such as event detection and event recognition. A Python library for WikiBio event processing is publicly available at Github: https://github.com/straniscimarco/wikibio. The main contributions of this paper are as follows: 1. the construction of a large-scale, event-based corpus derived from Wikipedia pages using a set of event extraction and pattern matching techniques; 2. the analysis of the relationships between event data in terms of semantic similarity and frequency of appearance, providing insights on event intersections and co-occurrences; 3. the demonstration of WikiBio as a resource for automatic event detection and classification tasks, using state-of-the-art machine learning algorithms. The presented resource can be further enriched with additional metadata about individual events, entities, and biographies. Additional information and resources about WikiBio are available at https://github.com/straniscimarco/wikibio-website. title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.

title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.
question: quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" ècrit par Ozan Caglayan et al., quels sont les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences ?
output_text: Dans l'article "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" écrit par Ozan Caglayan et al., les abandons choisis mentionnés dans les hyperparamètres pour les expériences sont les suivants :1. Le nombre d'époques : le nombre d'itérations pendant lesquelles le modèle apprend à partir des données d'entraînement.2. La taille du lot : la taille de la quantité de données utilisée pour chaque itération d'apprentissage.3. La régularisation : une technique utilisée pour éviter que le modèle ne s'ajuste trop aux données d'entraînement et ne suradapte.5. La diversité des données d'entraînement : la variété des données utilisées pour entraîner le modèle.10. La quantité de données de validation : la quantité de données utilisées pour évaluer la performance du modèle pendant l'entraînement.Ces hyperparamètres ont été ajustés pour différentes expériences afin de déterminer l'impact de chaque paramètre sur la performance du modèle multimodal.<|im_end|>

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.
question: Comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" ècrit par Alon Talmor et al., comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
output_text: Dans l'article "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" écrit par Alon Talmor et al., la précision humaine a été testée en demandant à un groupe de personnes de répondre aux mêmes questions que celles posées au modèle de langage. Les réponses humaines ont ensuite été comparées aux réponses du modèle pour déterminer la précision du modèle.Le groupe de personnes qui ont répondu aux questions était composé de 100 personnes, qui ont été sélectionnées pour représenter une gamme diversifiée de personnes en termes de langue maternelle, de niveau d'éducation et de profession. Les participants ont été invités à répondre aux questions en utilisant leur propre connaissance et leur compréhension du monde réel.Les questions posées aux participants étaient les mêmes que celles posées au modèle de langage, et les réponses ont été évaluées en fonction de leur pertinence et de leur précision. Les résultats ont montré que le modèle de langage avait une précision légèrement supérieure à celle des participants humains, avec une précision moyenne de 84,4% pour le modèle contre 82,2% pour les participants humains.Cependant, il convient de noter que la précision humaine peut varier en fonction de nombreux facteurs, tels que la langue maternelle, le niveau d'éducation et la profession des participants. De plus, la précision humaine peut également être influencée par des biais ou des préjugés personnels.<|im_end|>

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.
question: Quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" ècrit par Nitika Mathur et al., quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
output_text: Les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l'évaluation dans l'article "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" sont :1. BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) : c'est une mesure de référence couramment utilisée pour évaluer la qualité de la traduction automatique. Elle mesure la similarité entre une traduction et un corpus de référence en calculant la proportion de sous-chaînes de mots identiques dans la traduction et le corpus de référence.2. METEOR : c'est une mesure qui combine la précision et le rappel en calculant le score de similarité entre une traduction et un corpus de référence en utilisant à la fois des mots et des phrases.3. ROUGE (ROUge and ROUge Lite) : c'est une mesure qui calcule la similarité entre une traduction et un corpus de référence en utilisant des sous-chaînes de mots et des phrases. Elle est similaire à BLEU, mais elle prend en compte la longueur des sous-chaînes et les ordres de mots.Ces mesures sont utilisées pour évaluer la qualité de la traduction automatique en comparant la traduction avec un corpus de référence, qui est un ensemble de traductions humaines de haute qualité.<|im_end|>

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.

output_text: Les données de mouvements oculaires utilisées dans l'article sont appelées "Eye Tracking Data".<|im_end|>

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.

Les modèles NLP sont des modèles qui sont entraînés sur des données naturelles, telles que des textes en langage courant, tandis que les modèles UNNLP sont entraînés sur des données non naturelles, telles que des textes générés par des humains ou des textes générés par des machines.<|im_end|>

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.

Ce corpus contient des paires de phrases en anglais et en hindi, avec des étiquettes indiquant la langue de chaque phrase.<|im_end|>

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.

Ils suggèrent également que l'utilisation de techniques d'apprentissage par renforcement pourrait être une approche prometteuse pour améliorer les performances des modèles de NLP. title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.

Bender et al., ils pré-entraînent un modèle pour une prédiction binarisée de la phrase suivante afin de déterminer si la phrase suivante est une question ou non. Cela permet au modèle de mieux comprendre le contexte et le sens de la phrase, ce qui est important pour la compréhension du langage naturel (NLU).<|im_end|>

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.

title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.

output_text: article, and we propose a novel architecture that leverages the power of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to learn phonological representations. We use a CNN to extract features from the input audio signal, and then use a GAN to generate phonological representations from these features. We also use a CNN to classify the generated phonological representations. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of French and English speech, and show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods for phonological representation learning.<|im_end|>

title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.
