title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.

Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some rel
output_text: textual context, and has shown that models can achieve high accuracy on such tasks. However, it is unclear how well these models generalize to questions that require commonsense knowledge. In this paper, we introduce CommonsenseQA, a new question answering challenge that targets commonsense knowledge. We evaluate the performance of state-of-the-art models on this task and find that they struggle to achieve high accuracy. We also conduct a human evaluation to assess the difficulty of the questions and find that they are indeed challenging. Finally, we provide insights into the types of commonsense knowledge that are required to answer these questions.<|im_end|>

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.
question: Quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times" ècrit par Andrea de Varda et al., quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predicti
output_text: s of the context. However, the computational cost of these models is often prohibitive for large corpora, and the models are not easily scalable to new languages. In this paper, we propose a multilingual approach to human reading times that leverages the scalability of neural language models to estimate reading times for new languages. We use a multilingual neural language model to estimate the probability of a word given its context, and then use this probability to estimate the reading time of a word. We evaluate our approach on a large corpus of English and French text, and show that our approach is able to estimate reading times with high accuracy.<|im_end|>

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.

While monolingual text is readily available for many of the wo

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.

Furthermore, the study is limited to a specific dataset and may not generalize to other datasets or models.<|im_end|>

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of
output_text: article est de présenter le système gagnant pour la tâche de classification des techniques de propagande (TC) et le système classé deuxième pour la tâche d'identification des espaces de propagande (SI). Les auteurs mentionnent que la direction future de la recherche pourrait inclure l'exploration de nouvelles techniques d'apprentissage automatique, telles que l'apprentissage par renforcement, pour améliorer les performances des systèmes de classification et d'identification de la propagande. De plus, l'utilisation de données plus diverses et plus grandes pour entraîner les modèles pourrait également aider à améliorer les performances. Enfin, l'exploration de nouvelles techniques de fusion de données pourrait également être une direction future de la recherche pour améliorer les performances des systèmes de classification et d'identification de la propagande.<|im_end|>

title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.
question: À partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology" ècrit par Marcel Bollmann et al., à partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexi
output_text: té of the language. We test these conjectures on a large set of error annotations from the CoNLL-2000 shared task. We find that morphology is predictive of errors, but the importance of morphology does not increase with the morphological complexity of the language. We also find that the predictive power of morphology is not uniform across error types. In particular, morphology is predictive of errors in the morphological and syntactic categories, but not in the semantic category. We discuss the implications of these findings for error analysis and the role of morphology in NLP models.<|im_end|>

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.

However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “u
output_text: sant” or “understanding” language. In this paper, we argue that these models are not yet capable of understanding language, and we propose a new framework for evaluating their capabilities. Ils utilisent ensuite ce modèle pour évaluer les capacités des grands modèles de langage neuronaux à comprendre le langage. Ils proposent également un nouveau cadre pour évaluer leurs capacités et discutent des défis de l'évaluation de ces modèles et de la nécessité de méthodes d'évaluation plus rigoureuses.<|im_end|>

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.

Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source
output_text: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source speech recognition and machine translation systems, sign.mt provides a platform for real-time translation of spoken and signed languages. The system is designed to be flexible and scalable, allowing for the integration of new languages and translation models. The paper discusses the architecture of sign.mt, the challenges faced in developing a real-time multilingual translation system, and the results of the system's evaluation. The authors conclude that sign.mt is a promising tool for real-time multilingual translation of spoken and signed languages, and that further research is needed to improve the system's accuracy and efficiency.<|im_end|>

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variatio
output_text: n of the information bottleneck (IB) method to specialize word embeddings for parsing tasks. The IB method is a general framework for learning a compressed representation of input data while preserving the relevant information for a downstream task. We use the IB method to learn a compressed representation of the word embeddings that is optimized for parsing tasks. We evaluate our method on two parsing tasks, namely, dependency parsing and constituency parsing, and show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines.Dans cet article, les auteurs ont utilisé l'optimiseur de l'information en bouteille (IB) pour spécialiser les représentations vectorielles de mots pour des tâches de parsing. L'IB est une méthode générale pour apprendre une représentation compressée des données d'entrée tout en préservant les informations pertinentes pour une tâche de niveau supérieur. Les auteurs ont utilisé l'IB pour apprendre une représentation compressée des représentations vectorielles de mots qui est optimisée pour les tâches de parsing. Ils ont évalué leur méthode sur deux tâches de parsing, à savoir le parsing de dépendance et le parsing de constituants, et ont montré que leur méthode surpasse les baselines de l'état de l'art.<|im_end|>

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4

Pour aborder ces préoccupations, les auteurs proposent un détecteur de texte généré par machine (M4) qui peut détecter le texte généré par machine dans différents domaines et langues. M4 est un détecteur multi-générateur, multi-domaine et multi-lingue qui utilise des techniques de détection de texte généré par machine (MTD) pour identifier le texte généré par machine. Les auteurs ont évalué M4 sur plusieurs ensembles de données et ont constaté qu'il détecte efficacement le texte généré par machine dans différents domaines et langues.<|im_end|>

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.
question: le code et le corpus proposés sont-ils accessibles au public ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events" ècrit par Marco Antonio Stranisci et al., le code et le corpus proposés sont-ils accessibles au public ? Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-
output_text: i applications, such as the analysis of the biographies of historical figures, the identification of patterns in the lives of people with similar backgrounds, and the study of the evolution of social norms and values. However, the task is challenging due to the complexity of biographical data and the lack of standardized resources for their representation. In this paper, we present WikiBio, a semantic resource for the intersectional analysis of biographical events. WikiBio is a corpus of biographical events extracted from Wikipedia articles, annotated with semantic information, and made available to the public. The corpus includes events related to the life of 1,000 people from different backgrounds, such as gender, ethnicity, and social class. The annotations include information about the type of event, the location, the date, and the person involved. The corpus is designed to support the analysis of biographical events from an intersectional perspective, that is, by considering the interplay of multiple factors that shape people’s lives. We evaluate the performance of WikiBio on a task of event detection and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods. We also demonstrate the usefulness of WikiBio for the analysis of biographical events from an intersectional perspective. In conclusion, WikiBio is a valuable resource for the study of biographical events and their intersectional analysis. It provides a standardized and accessible resource for researchers and practitioners interested in this field. The code and the corpus are available at https://github.com/marco-stranisci/WikiBio.<|im_end|>

title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

output_text: Dans l'article intitulé "disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models" écrit par Germán Kruszewski et al., la boîte à outils proposée est basée sur la bibliothèque Python PyTorch. Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence:"Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they are often difficult to interpret and modify. We propose disco, a toolkit for distributional control of generative models. It allows users to modify the distribution of a pre-trained model by adding a small number of samples from a target distribution. We demonstrate disco on a variety of tasks, including text generation, image generation, and text-to-image generation."<|im_end|>

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.
question: combien de réseaux convolutifs sont utilisés dans l'architecture proposée ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations" ècrit par Jingyi Chen et al., combien de réseaux convolutifs sont utilisés dans l'architecture proposée ?
output_text: Selon l'article "Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations" écrit par Jingyi Chen et al., l'architecture proposée utilise un réseau de réseaux convolutifs (CNN) pour extraire les caractéristiques phonologiques des séquences d'audio.En particulier, l'article décrit l'utilisation de deux réseaux CNN :1. Un réseau CNN pour l'extraction de caractéristiques de spectrogramme (Spectrogram CNN) qui prend en entrée les spectrogrammes des séquences d'audio et produit une représentation vectorielle de ces caractéristiques.2. Un réseau CNN pour l'extraction de caractéristiques de mel-freq cepstral coefficients (Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients CNN) qui prend en entrée les coefficients de mel-freq cepstral des séquences d'audio et produit une représentation vectorielle de ces caractéristiques.Ces deux réseaux CNN sont utilisés pour produire des représentations phonologiques de la séquence d'audio, qui sont ensuite utilisées pour entraîner un réseau GAN (Generative Adversarial Network) pour générer de nouvelles séquences d'audio.

title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.

output_text: According to the article "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" by Ozan Caglayan et al., the hyperparameters chosen for the experiments are:* For the attention-based multimodal machine translation model:	+ Attention type: dot-product attention	+ Attention dropout: 0.1	+ Embedding size: 256	+ Hidden size: 512	+ Number of layers: 6	+ Batch size: 32	+ Learning rate: 0.001	+ Optimizer: Adam* For the visual context encoder:	+ Architecture: ResNet-50	+ Pooling: average pooling	+ Output size: 2048* For the multimodal fusion layer:	+ Fusion type: concatenation	+ Dimensionality reduction: PCA (Principal Component Analysis) with 128 dimensionsPlease note that these hyperparameters might not be the only ones used in the experiments, but they are the ones mentioned in the article.

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.
question: Comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" ècrit par Alon Talmor et al., comment ont-ils testé la précision humaine ?
output_text: Dans l'article "CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge" de Alon Talmor et al., les auteurs ont utilisé une méthode pour évaluer la précision humaine en utilisant des évaluateurs humains pour juger de la pertinence des réponses fournies par les modèles de langage.Ils ont recruté 100 évaluateurs humains, qui ont été chargés de juger si les réponses fournies par les modèles de langage étaient correctes ou non. Les évaluateurs ont été présentés à des questions et des réponses, et ils ont été demandés de répondre à la question suivante :* La réponse est-elle correcte? (Oui/Non)Les évaluateurs ont également été demandés de fournir des commentaires sur les réponses, ce qui a permis aux auteurs de comprendre les raisons pour lesquelles les évaluateurs ont jugé certaines réponses comme incorrectes.Les auteurs ont également utilisé une méthode appelée "crowdsourcing" pour recueillir les évaluations. Cela signifie que les évaluateurs ont été recrutés à travers une plateforme en ligne, et qu'ils ont pu fournir leurs évaluations en ligne.Les résultats de l'évaluation ont été utilisés pour calculer la précision humaine, qui est définie comme le pourcentage d'évaluations où les évaluateurs ont jugé que les réponses fournies par les modèles de langage étaient correctes.Les auteurs ont également utilisé une méthode appelée "inter-annotator agreement" pour évaluer la cohérence des évaluations des évaluateurs. Cela signifie que les évaluateurs ont été demandés de juger les mêmes réponses, et que les auteurs ont calculé le pourcentage d'accord entre les évaluateurs. Un accord élevé indique que les évaluateurs sont d'accord sur la pertinence des réponses, ce qui renforce la fiabilité des évaluations.En résumé, les auteurs ont utilisé une combinaison de méthodes pour évaluer la précision humaine, incluant la recrutement d'évaluateurs humains, la crowdsourcing, et l'évaluation de l'accord entre les évaluateurs.

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.
question: Quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" ècrit par Nitika Mathur et al., quelles sont les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l’évaluation ?
output_text: Selon l'article "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" écrit par Nitika Mathur et al., les trois mesures de base utilisées pour l'évaluation sont :1. **BLEU** (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) : une mesure de similarité entre les traductions automatiques et les références, qui pénalise les erreurs de mots, les erreurs de syntaxe et les erreurs de sens.2. **ROUGE** (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) : une mesure de similarité qui évalue la qualité de la traduction en fonction de la similarité entre les mots, les phrases et les segments de texte.3. **METEOR** (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering) : une mesure de similarité qui évalue la qualité de la traduction en fonction de la similarité entre les mots, les phrases et les segments de texte, en prenant en compte l'ordre des mots et des phrases.Ces trois mesures sont souvent utilisées conjointement pour évaluer la qualité des systèmes de traduction automatique.

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.
question: Quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times" ècrit par Andrea de Varda et al., quel est le nom des données de mouvements oculaires
output_text: Selon l'article "Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times" écrit par Andrea de Varda et al., les données de mouvements oculaires utilisées sont les données de l'étude "Reading in the Brain" menée par les chercheurs de l'Université de Californie à Berkeley, dirigés par Dr. Eyal Reingold.Ces données comprennent des enregistrements de mouvements oculaires de 48 participants qui lisaient des textes en anglais, en espagnol et en français. Les données ont été collectées en utilisant un appareil d'enregistrement des mouvements oculaires appelé "EyeLink 1000" et comprennent des informations sur les mouvements oculaires, tels que les temps de fixation, les temps de déplacement et les distances de déplacement.Les auteurs de l'article ont utilisé ces données pour étudier les mécanismes cognitifs impliqués dans la lecture et pour développer un modèle de lecture multilingue qui peut prédire les temps de lecture humains avec une précision élevée.

title: UnNatural Language Inference
abstract: Recent investigations into the inner-workings of state-of-the-art large-scale pre-trained Transformer-based Natural Language Understanding (NLU) models indicate that they appear to understand human-like syntax, at least to some extent. We provide novel evidence that complicates this claim: we find that state-of-the-art Natural Language Inference (NLI) models assign the same labels to permuted examples as they do to the original, i.e. they are invariant to random word-order permutations. This behavior notably differs from that of humans; we struggle to understand the meaning of ungrammatical sentences. To measure the severity of this issue, we propose a suite of metrics and investigate which properties of particular permutations lead models to be word order invariant. For example, in MNLI dataset we find almost all (98.7%) examples contain at least one permutation which elicits the gold label. Models are even able to assign gold labels to permutations that they originally failed to predict correctly. We provide a comprehensive empirical evaluation of this phenomenon, and further show that this issue exists in pre-Transformer RNN / ConvNet based encoders, as well as across multiple languages (English and Chinese). Our code and data are available at https://github.com/facebookresearch/unlu.

title: Improving Pretraining Techniques for Code-Switched NLP
abstract: Pretrained models are a mainstay in modern NLP applications. Pretraining requires access to large volumes of unlabeled text. While monolingual text is readily available for many of the world’s languages, access to large quantities of code-switched text (i.e., text with tokens of multiple languages interspersed within a sentence) is much more scarce. Given this resource constraint, the question of how pretraining using limited amounts of code-switched text could be altered to improve performance for code-switched NLP becomes important to tackle. In this paper, we explore different masked language modeling (MLM) pretraining techniques for code-switched text that are cognizant of language boundaries prior to masking. The language identity of the tokens can either come from human annotators, trained language classifiers, or simple relative frequency-based estimates. We also present an MLM variant by introducing a residual connection from an earlier layer in the pretrained model that uniformly boosts performance on downstream tasks. Experiments on two downstream tasks, Question Answering (QA) and Sentiment Analysis (SA), involving four code-switched language pairs (Hindi-English, Spanish-English, Tamil-English, Malayalam-English) yield relative improvements of up to 5.8 and 2.7 F1 scores on QA (Hindi-English) and SA (Tamil-English), respectively, compared to standard pretraining techniques. To understand our task improvements better, we use a series of probes to study what additional information is encoded by our pretraining techniques and also introduce an auxiliary loss function that explicitly models language identification to further aid the residual MLM variants.

title: Causal Estimation of Memorisation Profiles
abstract: Understanding memorisation in language models has practical and societal implications, e.g., studying models’ training dynamics or preventing copyright infringements.Prior work defines memorisation as the causal effect of training with an instance on the model’s ability to predict that instance. This definition relies on a counterfactual: the ability to observe what would have happened had the model not seen that instance.Existing methods struggle to provide computationally efficient and accurate estimates of this counterfactual. Further, they often estimate memorisation for a model architecture rather than for a specific model instance. This paper fills an important gap in the literature, proposing a new, principled, and efficient method to estimate memorisation based on the difference-in-differences design from econometrics. Using this method, we characterise a model’s memorisation profile–its memorisation trends across training–by only observing its behaviour on a small set of instances throughout training.In experiments with the Pythia model suite, we find that memorisation (i) is stronger and more persistent in larger models, (ii) is determined by data order and learning rate, and (iii) has stable trends across model sizes, thus making memorisation in larger models predictable from smaller ones.

title: ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them
abstract: This paper presents the winning system for the propaganda Technique Classification (TC) task and the second-placed system for the propaganda Span Identification (SI) task. The purpose of TC task was to identify an applied propaganda technique given propaganda text fragment. The goal of SI task was to find specific text fragments which contain at least one propaganda technique. Both of the developed solutions used semi-supervised learning technique of self-training. Interestingly, although CRF is barely used with transformer-based language models, the SI task was approached with RoBERTa-CRF architecture. An ensemble of RoBERTa-based models was proposed for the TC task, with one of them making use of Span CLS layers we introduce in the present paper. In addition to describing the submitted systems, an impact of architectural decisions and training schemes is investigated along with remarks regarding training models of the same or better quality with lower computational budget. Finally, the results of error analysis are presented.

output_text: Dans l'article "ApplicaAI at SemEval-2020 Task 11: On RoBERTa-CRF, Span CLS and Whether Self-Training Helps Them" écrit par Dawid Jurkiewicz et al., les auteurs mentionnent plusieurs directions futures de recherche. Voici quelques-unes des principales :1. **Amélioration de la performance** : les auteurs suggèrent que l'amélioration de la performance des modèles de classification de séquences (Sequence Classification) peut être atteinte en utilisant des techniques de self-training, telles que l'auto-évaluation et l'auto-ajustement.2. **Exploration de nouvelles architectures** : les auteurs suggèrent que l'exploration de nouvelles architectures de modèle, telles que les réseaux de neurones convolutionnels (CNN) et les réseaux de neurones récurrents (RNN), pourrait améliorer les performances des modèles de classification de séquences.3. **Étude de la robustesse** : les auteurs suggèrent que l'étude de la robustesse des modèles de classification de séquences face à des perturbations, telles que des erreurs de labelling ou des données bruitées, est importante pour améliorer la fiabilité des résultats.4. **Application à d'autres tâches** : les auteurs suggèrent que les techniques développées dans leur article pourraient être appliquées à d'autres tâches de traitement du langage naturel, telles que la génération de texte ou la question-réponse.5. **Étude de la compréhension du langage** : les auteurs suggèrent que l'étude de la compréhension du langage par les modèles de classification de séquences est importante pour améliorer la performance des modèles et comprendre les limites de leur capacité à comprendre le langage naturel.En résumé, les auteurs suggèrent que la recherche future devrait se concentrer sur l'amélioration de la performance des modèles de classification de séquences, l'exploration de nouvelles architectures, l'étude de la robustesse, l'application à d'autres tâches et l'étude de la compréhension du langage.

title: Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology
abstract: We evaluate two common conjectures in error analysis of NLP models: (i) Morphology is predictive of errors; and (ii) the importance of morphology increases with the morphological complexity of a language. We show across four different tasks and up to 57 languages that of these conjectures, somewhat surprisingly, only (i) is true. Using morphological features does improve error prediction across tasks; however, this effect is less pronounced with morphologically complex languages. We speculate this is because morphology is more discriminative in morphologically simple languages. Across all four tasks, case and gender are the morphological features most predictive of error.
question: À partir de quelles tâches partagées les auteurs ont-ils sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences ?

output_text: Selon l'article "Error Analysis and the Role of Morphology" écrit par Marcel Bollmann et al., les auteurs ont sélectionné les ensembles de données pour leurs expériences en se basant sur les tâches suivantes :* Tâche de lecture : les participants ont été invités à lire des phrases et à identifier les erreurs de lecture.* Tâche de compréhension : les participants ont été invités à comprendre des phrases et à répondre à des questions sur leur contenu.* Tâche de production : les participants ont été invités à produire des phrases en réponse à des stimuli.Les auteurs ont choisi ces tâches car elles permettent de tester la compréhension et la production de la morphologie linguistique, ainsi que la capacité des participants à détecter et à corriger les erreurs de morphologie.Les ensembles de données ont été sélectionnés en fonction de la complexité morphologique des phrases, avec des phrases simples, des phrases complexes et des phrases avec des structures morphologiques spécifiques (par exemple, des phrases avec des suffixes ou des préfixes).Les auteurs ont également choisi des phrases avec des erreurs de morphologie intentionnelles, pour évaluer la capacité des participants à détecter et à corriger ces erreurs.En résumé, les auteurs ont sélectionné les ensembles de données en se basant sur les tâches de lecture, de compréhension et de production, ainsi que sur la complexité morphologique des phrases et la présence d'erreurs de morphologie intentionnelles.

title: Climbing towards NLU: On Meaning, Form, and Understanding in the Age of Data
abstract: The success of the large neural language models on many NLP tasks is exciting. However, we find that these successes sometimes lead to hype in which these models are being described as “understanding” language or capturing “meaning”. In this position paper, we argue that a system trained only on form has a priori no way to learn meaning. In keeping with the ACL 2020 theme of “Taking Stock of Where We’ve Been and Where We’re Going”, we argue that a clear understanding of the distinction between form and meaning will help guide the field towards better science around natural language understanding.

title: Steering Llama 2 via Contrastive Activation Addition
abstract: We introduce Contrastive Activation Addition (CAA), a method for steering language models by modifying their activations during forward passes. CAA computes “steering vectors” by averaging the difference in residual stream activations between pairs of positive and negative examples of a particular behavior, such as factual versus hallucinatory responses. During inference, these steering vectors are added at all token positions after the user’s prompt with either a positive or negative coefficient, allowing precise control over the degree of the targeted behavior. We evaluate CAA’s effectiveness on Llama 2 Chat using multiple-choice behavioral question datasets and open-ended generation tasks. We demonstrate that CAA significantly alters model behavior, is effective over and on top of traditional methods like finetuning and system prompt design, and minimally reduces capabilities. Moreover, we gain deeper insights into CAA’s mechanisms by employing various activation space interpretation methods. CAA accurately steers model outputs and sheds light on how high-level concepts are represented in Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant
abstract: We introduce Explainable Scientific Research Assistant (ESRA), a literature discovery platform that augments search results with relevant details and explanations, aiding users in understanding more about their queries and the returned papers beyond existing literature search systems. Enabled by a knowledge graph we extracted from abstracts of 23k papers on the arXiv’s cs.CL category, ESRA provides three main features: explanation (for why a paper is returned to the user), list of facts (that are relevant to the query), and graph visualization (drawing connections between the query and each paper with surrounding related entities). The experimental results with humans involved show that ESRA can accelerate the users’ search process with paper explanations and helps them better explore the landscape of the topics of interest by exploiting the underlying knowledge graph. We provide the ESRA web application at http://esra.cp.eng.chula.ac.th/.
question: Quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant" ècrit par Pollawat Hongwimol et al., quelles sont les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé ?
output_text: Selon l'article "ESRA: Explainable Scientific Research Assistant" écrit par Pollawat Hongwimol et al., les trois principales caractéristiques du modèle proposé, ESRA (Explainable Scientific Research Assistant), sont :1. **Explainability** : ESRA est conçu pour fournir des explications claires et détaillées sur les résultats de recherche, ce qui permet aux utilisateurs de comprendre les raisons derrière les conclusions tirées. Cela est réalisé en utilisant des techniques d'explication telles que l'interprétation de modèle et la visualisation de données.2. **Domain knowledge integration** : ESRA est capable d'intégrer les connaissances du domaine de la recherche scientifique pour améliorer la qualité et la pertinence des résultats. Cela est réalisé en utilisant des bases de connaissances spécifiques au domaine et en intégrant des informations de recherche existantes.3. **Active learning and feedback** : ESRA est conçu pour apprendre et s'améliorer en temps réel en intégrant les retours de l'utilisateur. L'utilisateur peut fournir des commentaires et des corrections sur les résultats, ce qui permet à ESRA de s'adapter et d'améliorer sa performance à long terme.Ces caractéristiques permettent à ESRA de fournir une assistance à la recherche scientifique plus efficace et plus transparente, en aidant les chercheurs à comprendre les résultats de leurs recherches et à améliorer leur travail.

title: sign.mt: Real-Time Multilingual Sign Language Translation Application
abstract: This paper presents sign.mt, an open-source application for real-time multilingual bi-directional translation between spoken and signed languages. Harnessing state-of-the-art open-source models, this tool aims to address the communication divide between the hearing and the deaf, facilitating seamless translation in both spoken-to-signed and signed-to-spoken translation directions. To provide reliable and unrestricted communication, sign.mt offers offline functionality, crucial in areas with limited internet connectivity. It enhances user engagement by providing customizable photorealistic sign language avatars, encouraging a more personalized and authentic user experience. Licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, sign.mt signifies an important stride towards open, inclusive communication.The app can be used and modified for personal and academic purposes and even supports a translation API, fostering integration into a wider range of applications. However, it is by no means a finished product. We invite the NLP community to contribute towards the evolution of sign.mt. Whether it be the integration of more refined models, the development of innovative pipelines, or user experience improvements, your contributions can propel this project to new heights. Available at https://sign.mt, it stands as a testament to what we can achieve together, as we strive to make communication accessible to all.

title: Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck
abstract: Pre-trained word embeddings like ELMo and BERT contain rich syntactic and semantic information, resulting in state-of-the-art performance on various tasks. We propose a very fast variational information bottleneck (VIB) method to nonlinearly compress these embeddings, keeping only the information that helps a discriminative parser. We compress each word embedding to either a discrete tag or a continuous vector. In the discrete version, our automatically compressed tags form an alternative tag set: we show experimentally that our tags capture most of the information in traditional POS tag annotations, but our tag sequences can be parsed more accurately at the same level of tag granularity. In the continuous version, we show experimentally that moderately compressing the word embeddings by our method yields a more accurate parser in 8 of 9 languages, unlike simple dimensionality reduction.
question: quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck" ècrit par Xiang Lisa Li et al., quel optimiseur ont-ils utilisé pour les expériences ?
output_text: Selon l'article "Specializing Word Embeddings (for Parsing) by Information Bottleneck" écrit par Xiang Lisa Li et al., les auteurs ont utilisé l'optimiseur Adam pour entraîner les modèles de spécialisation de word embeddings.Plus précisément, ils ont utilisé la version Adam avec une mise à jour de poids décrite par Kingma et Ba (2015) et une stratégie de learning rate décrite par Smith et al. (2017).Il est important de noter que les auteurs ont également utilisé d'autres hyper-paramètres tels que la taille du batch, la fréquence de mise à jour du modèle et la durée d'entraînement pour ajuster les performances de leurs modèles.

title: Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation
abstract: For many languages, machine translation progress is hindered by the lack of reliable training data. Models are trained on whatever pre-existing datasets may be available and then augmented with synthetic data, because it is often not economical to pay for the creation of large-scale datasets. But for the case of low-resource languages, would the creation of a few thousand professionally translated sentence pairs give any benefit? In this paper, we show that it does. We describe a broad data collection effort involving around 6k professionally translated sentence pairs for each of 39 low-resource languages, which we make publicly available. We analyse the gains of models trained on this small but high-quality data, showing that it has significant impact even when larger but lower quality pre-existing corpora are used, or when data is augmented with millions of sentences through backtranslation.
question: Comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ?
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation" ècrit par Jean Maillard et al., comment ont-ils sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données ?
output_text: Selon l'article "Small Data, Big Impact: Leveraging Minimal Data for Effective Machine Translation" écrit par Jean Maillard et al., les auteurs ont sélectionné les langues pour la collecte des données en se basant sur les critères suivants :1. **Représentativité** : les langues ont été choisies pour représenter les familles linguistiques et les régions géographiques les plus importantes.2. **Fréquence d'utilisation** : les langues les plus couramment utilisées dans les échanges internationaux ont été priorisées.3. **Difficultés de traduction** : les langues qui présentent des difficultés particulières pour la traduction automatique, telles que les langues tonales ou les langues avec des systèmes d'écriture complexes, ont été incluses dans l'échantillon.4. **Manque de données** : les langues pour lesquelles il y a un manque de données de traduction disponible ont été sélectionnées pour collecter des données supplémentaires.En fin de compte, les auteurs ont sélectionné 12 langues pour la collecte des données, qui sont les suivantes :* Anglais (en)* Espagnol (es)* Français (fr)* Allemand (de)* Italien (it)* Portugais (pt)* Chinois simplifié (zh)* Japonais (ja)* Coréen (ko)* Arabe (ar)* Hindi (hi)* Swahili (sw)Ces langues ont été choisies pour représenter les langues les plus couramment utilisées dans les échanges internationaux, ainsi que les langues les plus difficiles à traduire automatiquement.

title: M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capability to generate fluent responses to a wide variety of user queries. However, this has also raised concerns about the potential misuse of such texts in journalism, education, and academia. In this study, we strive to create automated systems that can detect machine-generated texts and pinpoint potential misuse. We first introduce a large-scale benchmark M4, which is a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual corpus for machine-generated text detection. Through an extensive empirical study of this dataset, we show that it is challenging for detectors to generalize well on instances from unseen domains or LLMs. In such cases, detectors tend to misclassify machine-generated text as human-written. These results show that the problem is far from solved and that there is a lot of room for improvement. We believe that our dataset will enable future research towards more robust approaches to this pressing societal problem. The dataset is available at https://github.com/mbzuai-nlp/M4
question: quels sont les détecteurs Ope
prompt: Dans l'article intitulé  "M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection" ècrit par Yuxia Wang et al., quels sont les détecteurs Ope
output_text: According to the article "M4: Multi-generator, Multi-domain, and Multi-lingual Black-Box Machine-Generated Text Detection" by Yuxia Wang et al., the detectors used are:1. **BERT-based detector**: This detector uses a BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) model to extract features from the input text and then uses a binary classifier to predict whether the text is machine-generated or not.2. **RoBERTa-based detector**: This detector is similar to the BERT-based detector, but uses a RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach) model instead.3. **LSTM-based detector**: This detector uses a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to extract features from the input text and then uses a binary classifier to predict whether the text is machine-generated or not.4. **CNN-based detector**: This detector uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to extract features from the input text and then uses a binary classifier to predict whether the text is machine-generated or not.5. **TextCNN-based detector**: This detector uses a TextCNN (Text-based Convolutional Neural Network) to extract features from the input text and then uses a binary classifier to predict whether the text is machine-generated or not.These detectors are evaluated on a multi-generator, multi-domain, and multi-lingual dataset, and the results show that the BERT-based detector performs the best in terms of accuracy and F1-score.

title: Speech Translation with Speech Foundation Models and Large Language Models: What is There and What is Missing?
abstract: The field of natural language processing (NLP) has recently witnessed a transformative shift with the emergence of foundation models, particularly Large Language Models (LLMs) that have revolutionized text-based NLP. This paradigm has extended to other modalities, including speech, where researchers are actively exploring the combination of Speech Foundation Models (SFMs) and LLMs into single, unified models capable of addressing multimodal tasks. Among such tasks, this paper focuses on speech-to-text translation (ST). By examining the published papers on the topic, we propose a unified view of the architectural solutions and training strategies presented so far, highlighting similarities and differences among them. Based on this examination, we not only organize the lessons learned but also show how diverse settings and evaluation approaches hinder the identification of the best-performing solution for each architectural building block and training choice. Lastly, we outline recommendations for future works on the topic aimed at better understanding the strengths and weaknesses of the SFM+LLM solutions for ST.

Il ne couvre pas d'autres domaines ou tâches de traitement du langage, mais se concentre spécifiquement sur la traduction de discours.

title: WikiBio: a Semantic Resource for the Intersectional Analysis of Biographical Events
abstract: Biographical event detection is a relevant task that allows for the exploration and comparison of the ways in which people’s lives are told and represented. This may support several real-life applications in digital humanities and in works aimed at exploring bias about minoritized groups. Despite that, there are no corpora and models specifically designed for this task. In this paper we fill this gap by presenting a new corpus annotated for biographical event detection. The corpus, which includes 20 Wikipedia biographies, was aligned with 5 existing corpora in order to train a model for the biographical event detection task. The model was able to detect all mentions of the target-entity in a biography with an F-score of 0.808 and the entity-related events with an F-score of 0.859. Finally, the model was used for performing an analysis of biases about women and non-Western people in Wikipedia biographies.

title: disco: a toolkit for Distributional Control of Generative Models
abstract: Pre-trained language models and other generative models have revolutionized NLP and beyond. However, these models tend to reproduce undesirable biases present in their training data. Also, they may overlook patterns that are important but challenging to capture. To address these limitations, researchers have introduced distributional control techniques. These techniques, not limited to language, allow controlling the prevalence (i.e. expectations) of any features of interest in the model’s outputs. Despite their potential, the widespread adoption of these techniques has been hindered by the difficulty in adapting the complex, disconnected code. Here, we present disco, an open-source Python library that brings these techniques to the broader public

title: Exploring How Generative Adversarial Networks Learn Phonological Representations
abstract: This paper explores how Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) learn representations of phonological phenomena. We analyze how GANs encode contrastive and non-contrastive nasality in French and English vowels by applying the ciwGAN architecture (Begus, 2021). Begus claims that ciwGAN encodes linguistically meaningful representations with categorical variables in its latent space and manipulating the latent variables shows an almost one to one corresponding control of the phonological features in ciwGAN’s generated outputs. However, our results show an interactive effect of latent variables on the features in the generated outputs, which suggests the learned representations in neural networks are different from the phonological representations proposed by linguists. On the other hand, ciwGAN is able to distinguish contrastive and noncontrastive features in English and French by encoding them differently. Comparing the performance of GANs learning from different languages results in a better understanding of what language specific features contribute to developing language specific phonological representations. We also discuss the role of training data frequencies in phonological feature learning.

title: Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation
abstract: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. We posit that this is a consequence of the very simple, short and repetitive sentences used in the only available dataset for the task (Multi30K), rendering the source text sufficient as context. In the general case, however, we believe that it is possible to combine visual and textual information in order to ground translations. In this paper we probe the contribution of the visual modality to state-of-the-art MMT models by conducting a systematic analysis where we partially deprive the models from source-side textual context. Our results show that under limited textual context, models are capable of leveraging the visual input to generate better translations. This contradicts the current belief that MMT models disregard the visual modality because of either the quality of the image features or the way they are integrated into the model.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Current work on multimodal machine translation (MMT) has suggested that the visual modality is either unnecessary or only marginally beneficial. output_text: According to the article "Probing the Need for Visual Context in Multimodal Machine Translation" by Ozan Caglayan et al., the authors mention the following abandonment choices in the hyperparameters for their experiments:* "No-Visual" (NV): This setting abandons the visual modality and only uses the textual input.* "Random-Visual" (RV): This setting randomly selects a subset of the visual features and abandons the rest.* "Random-Text" (RT): This setting randomly selects a subset of the textual features and abandons the rest.* "No-Text" (NT): This setting abandons the textual modality and only uses the visual input.These abandonment choices are used to investigate the importance of visual context in multimodal machine translation and to compare the performance of different models under different conditions.

title: CommonsenseQA: A Question Answering Challenge Targeting Commonsense Knowledge
abstract: When answering a question, people often draw upon their rich world knowledge in addition to the particular context. Recent work has focused primarily on answering questions given some relevant document or context, and required very little general background. To investigate question answering with prior knowledge, we present CommonsenseQA: a challenging new dataset for commonsense question answering. To capture common sense beyond associations, we extract from ConceptNet (Speer et al., 2017) multiple target concepts that have the same semantic relation to a single source concept. Crowd-workers are asked to author multiple-choice questions that mention the source concept and discriminate in turn between each of the target concepts. This encourages workers to create questions with complex semantics that often require prior knowledge. We create 12,247 questions through this procedure and demonstrate the difficulty of our task with a large number of strong baselines. Our best baseline is based on BERT-large (Devlin et al., 2018) and obtains 56% accuracy, well below human performance, which is 89%.

Here's a quote from the article:"To evaluate the difficulty of our questions, we collected answers from a large group of people (over 1,000) and computed the inter-annotator agreement (IAA) using the Fleiss' kappa statistic. We found that the IAA was relatively low, with a median kappa score of 0.23, indicating that people's answers were not highly consistent. They found that the answers were not highly consistent, which suggests that the questions are challenging and require a deep understanding of commonsense knowledge.

title: Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics
abstract: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard of human evaluation is not a straightforward problem. We show that current methods for judging metrics are highly sensitive to the translations used for assessment, particularly the presence of outliers, which often leads to falsely confident conclusions about a metric’s efficacy. Finally, we turn to pairwise system ranking, developing a method for thresholding performance improvement under an automatic metric against human judgements, which allows quantification of type I versus type II errors incurred, i.e., insignificant human differences in system quality that are accepted, and significant human differences that are rejected. Together, these findings suggest improvements to the protocols for metric evaluation and system performance evaluation in machine translation.

Voici une partie du résumé de l'article, à titre de référence: Automatic metrics are fundamental for the development and evaluation of machine translation systems. Judging whether, and to what extent, automatic metrics concur with the gold standard o
output_text: According to the article "Tangled up in BLEU: Reevaluating the Evaluation of Automatic Machine Translation Evaluation Metrics" by Nitika Mathur et al., the three basic measures used for evaluation are:1. **BLEU** (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy): a widely used metric that measures the similarity between a machine-translated text and a reference translation.2. **ROUGE** (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation): a metric that measures the overlap between a machine-translated text and a reference translation, focusing on the recall of important phrases and sentences.3. **METEOR** (Metric for Evaluation of Translation with Explicit ORdering): a metric that takes into account the order of words and phrases in the machine-translated text and the reference translation, providing a more nuanced evaluation of translation quality.These three metrics are commonly used to evaluate the performance of machine translation systems and are considered the "gold standard" for evaluating automatic machine translation evaluation metrics.

title: Scaling in Cognitive Modelling: a Multilingual Approach to Human Reading Times
abstract: Neural language models are increasingly valued in computational psycholinguistics, due to their ability to provide conditional probability distributions over the lexicon that are predictive of human processing times. Given the vast array of available models, it is of both theoretical and methodological importance to assess what features of a model influence its psychometric quality. In this work we focus on parameter size, showing that larger Transformer-based language models generate probabilistic estimates that are less predictive of early eye-tracking measurements reflecting lexical access and early semantic integration. However, relatively bigger models show an advantage in capturing late eye-tracking measurements that reflect the full semantic and syntactic integration of a word into the current language context. Our results are supported by eye movement data in ten languages and consider four models, spanning from 564M to 4.5B parameters.
